title: Entropy: Measuring Disorder
concept: Entropy is a mathematical metric that measures the amount of disorder or mixture in a dataset — high entropy means messy, mixed data, while zero entropy means a pure group.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a decision tree's root node?

options.text: The very first question at the top of the tree.
The final prediction returned at the end of a branch.

exercises.explain: Correct! The root node is the starting question of the tree.

cards.title: The messy toy box
cards.body: Imagine a toy box containing 50 red blocks and 50 blue blocks mixed together. If you close your eyes and pull one out, you have no idea what color you'll get. Now imagine a box containing 100 red blocks. You are 100% sure you'll get a red block. The first box is disordered; the second box is pure. We measure this disorder using a concept called entropy.

cards.title: Entropy: The metric of messiness
cards.body: Entropy is a score from 0 to 1. If a group is perfectly pure (e.g. all apples, or all bananas), its entropy is **0**. If a group is a perfect 50/50 mix, it is completely disordered, and its entropy is **1**.

cards.title: A clean room vs. a messy room
cards.body: Think of entropy like the state of a bedroom. If all clothes are hung up in the closet, the room is in a pure state — entropy is 0. If shirts, pants, and socks are thrown randomly across the floor, the room is disordered — entropy is high.

cards.title: Calculating the mix
cards.body: A dataset has 10 emails. If 5 are spam and 5 are normal, the mix is 50/50, so entropy is **1.0**. If 9 are spam and 1 is normal, the group is mostly sorted, so entropy drops to **0.47**. If all 10 are spam, the group is completely pure, so entropy is **0.0**.

cards.title: High entropy isn't 'good' or 'bad'
cards.body: It's easy to think high entropy is bad because it's 'disordered'. In reality, entropy is just a tool to help us see how mixed our data groups are. The goal of a decision tree is to split the data in a way that *reduces* entropy as much as possible, leading to pure leaves.

cards.title: Disorder on a Scale
cards.body: Entropy levels based on group mixtures:

left.text:
10 red marbles, 0 blue marbles.
100% pure. No disorder.
We know exactly what we will pick.

right.text:
5 red marbles, 5 blue marbles.
Perfect 50/50 mix. Maximum disorder.
Complete uncertainty.

exercises.prompt: If a group contains 100 items and every single item is a 'spam' email, what is the entropy of this group?

options.text: 0, because the group is completely pure.
1, because the group is perfectly mixed.

exercises.explain: Correct! A group containing only one category has zero disorder, so its entropy is exactly 0.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An entropy score of 1 represents the highest possible disorder, which happens when a group is split exactly 50/50 between two classes.
exercises.explain: True! Maximum uncertainty and mix occurs at a 50/50 split, giving an entropy of 1.

exercises.sentence: Entropy measures the amount of ___ or mixture in a group of data.
exercises.explain: Entropy is the mathematical metric used to quantify the mix or disorder of classes.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following marble bags has the HIGHEST entropy score?

options.text: Bag A: 10 green marbles, 10 yellow marbles
Bag B: 18 green marbles, 2 yellow marbles
Bag C: 20 green marbles, 0 yellow marbles

exercises.explain: Correct! Bag A has a perfect 50/50 mix, which represents maximum disorder and an entropy of 1.0.

exercises.prompt: You start with a bag of 50 red candies and 50 blue candies (Entropy = 1.0). You split them into two bowls. Bowl 1 contains 50 red candies. Bowl 2 contains 50 blue candies. What happened to the entropy of the system?

options.text: It dropped from 1.0 to 0.0 in both bowls, because each bowl is now completely pure.
It remained at 1.0, because the total candy count didn't change.
It increased to 2.0, because there are now two bowls.
It became negative because the red and blue candies were separated.

exercises.explain: Exactly! By separating the candies, you sorted the mixture. Each bowl now contains only one color, making them 100% pure (Entropy = 0). Next, let's look at another popular way trees measure this purity!
